explanation_md: `a(3)` uses the size constructor: it creates 3 elements all initialized to 0, so `a.size()` is 3. `b { 3 }` uses list initialization with the single value 3, producing a vector of 1 element, so `b.size()` is 1. The distinction between parentheses and braces is critical with `std::vector`.

prompt_md: Which declaration creates a `std::vector<double>` containing seven elements each set to `0.0`?
explanation_md: `v(7)` uses the size constructor: 7 default-initialized doubles (all 0.0). Option a creates a vector with one element whose value is 7. Option c does not compile. Option d works but requires writing 7 explicit zeros. The size constructor `v(N)` is the idiomatic solution.
`v.size()` returns 3 because the vector has three elements. `v[v.size() - 1]` is `v[2]`, which is the last element, 30. Using `v.size() - 1` is the correct way to access the last element.

prompt_md: What header must be included to use `std::vector`?
explanation_md: `std::vector` is defined in the `<vector>` header. `<array>` is for `std::array`, `<list>` is for `std::list`, and `<container>` does not exist as a standard header.
